goal: The app pre-builds your usual order based on your own purchase history — you just confirm it.

screens.name: Predicted Basket
screens.purpose: Surfaces the auto-generated weekly basket (usually via Sunday notification).

states.loading: n/a — basket is precomputed by a scheduled Cloud Function, not generated on open.
states.empty: Cold-start state (new user, <3 historical orders of any SKU): shows a category-based starter template instead, labeled 'Starter basket — we'll learn your pattern after a few orders.'
states.success: List of predicted items with per-item confidence chip (High/Medium), running total.
states.error: Prediction job failed silently upstream -> falls back to starter template, never shows a blank/broken basket.

screens.name: Why This Item?
screens.purpose: Explains why an item was predicted — builds trust in the algorithm.

states.success: 'You've bought this every ~12 days, last on [date]' — plain language, no jargon about the algorithm.

screens.name: Import Past Orders (Smart Reading)
screens.purpose: Backfills purchase history for users bringing habits from another app, via receipt OCR.

states.empty: 'Speed up your predictions — scan a few old receipts' — explicitly optional, skippable.
states.success: Parsed historical orders added to orders/{uid}, immediately improves next prediction cycle.

userFlow: Sunday morning: scheduled Cloud Function computes each SKU's purchase interval from order history.
User opens predicted_basket_home, sees milk/bread/eggs pre-filled with confidence chips.
Taps an item to see why it was included (confidence_detail).
Confirms -> proceeds to checkout with one tap instead of re-shopping from scratch.
edgeCases: New user with zero order history -> category starter template, not an empty screen.
SKU with wildly inconsistent purchase interval (high variance) -> excluded from prediction rather than guessed at.
User edits the same item every week -> treat as a signal to lower confidence / adjust interval, don't keep repeating the same wrong guess.
User skips several weeks in a row -> don't keep pushing notifications at the same cadence; back off.
notes: This is an interval-averaging heuristic, not a trained ML model — build the simple version first (avg days-between-purchase, gated by low variance). A real model is a later optimization, not a v1 requirement.
